speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. Today is May 31, 1996, and the per-- and our survivor is Mr. Sol Glick. As far as being a Jew, is there any kind of message that a Jew would like to, to tell them about Judaism, about [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he knows about Judaism.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I don't mean your son-in-law. I mean your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They go to the temple, and be a Jew, and a good Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all. And learn the, learn the Jewish history.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would-- what do you think your father would have liked to tell them if he would have survived to, to meet them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father would like to tell me?

speaker: interviewer
text: To tell them. If he would have seen his grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell them the same thing. Exactly the same thing. Raise them in the Jewish faith. And, and be good to people. And help people. And don't hate nobody. A human being is a human being. I was in a concentration with all nationalities. We lived like brothers. That's true. Hate is no good, any nationality.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So what do you want me to say? All right, tell us where the-- where the picture was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was made in Warsaw, where my brother got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] years-- about 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's there? Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture, to the left is my mother, sitting. And above my mother was my-- my oldest brother with his wife. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And, and later, my second brother, the, the, the second brother from my oldest with his, with his bride sitting. Above his bride and my brother was my picture. After my older brother is me. Next to my brother's-- to the bridegroom-- is my youngest brother, sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Shyike. ?] And next to him is my father, to the right-- to the end. Above my brother, above my father is my older brother from me and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name? Give the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zanwel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife was [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my mother when she got married. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name? What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivka. Rachel in English. In Jewish, it's Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And give us her maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alexander.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture when I settled in Cracow. I had to have a, a passport. And I took out the passport in Krakow after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is still before you changed your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Galek. Schlomo Galek. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This, this is on the field where we were playing a game. And we both jumped on top of the guy who wants to kick the ball with the head. And, and I was shorter than him, but I'm sitting on top of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Heidenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the DP camp? Say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a DP camp. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture when I came to America the first time in 1949 when I start for a photographer studio, when a teacher who teached photography took the picture of me, never retouched. That's how I looked before I start to work for the studio. They all called me Gene Kelly. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is my three brothers who survived the war, and they lived in America. And this was for my brother to the left, Zanwel, his son's [INAUDIBLE] bar mitzvah. And that's where we took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the brother to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the-- I, I am in the middle. To the right is my oldest brother, and to the left is my second brother after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the one to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one to the right is Zalmen, Zalmen [INAUDIBLE]. And to the left is Zanwel Galek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they spelled their name differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Galek. They spelled Galek here in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Different. Say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They're not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest brother with his wife, and his daughter, and, and, and, and his grandchild, and his son-in-law in the back. My oldest brother was Zalmen [INAUDIBLE]. His wife was [? Gitl ?] and the son-in-law was Jack. My niece was-- his daughter was [? Shelly. ?] And the little girl was [? Kara. ?] First grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about the spelling of Galek.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they spelled Galek like in the old county. G-A-L-E-K-.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the other brother spelled it differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other brother spelled G-A-L-E-C-K-.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you change your name to Glick?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I become a citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd say, I changed my name. I'm not on the mic, so you have to say it. You changed your name to Glick when you became a citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is me when I got married, my wedding picture. I made the picture, but I didn't photograph the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your wife, Miriam. Say this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Miriam. And my name is Sol Glick. But my name was Galek. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture for my nephew in the middle-- my brother who died. And to the-- and to the right is my older daughter, Lois. And to the left is my younger daughter, Brenda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the name of the nephew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert Galeck. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is the picture taken in mine apartment in California. My daughter came to visit me with my son-in-law and the baby. My daughter's name is Brenda, the boy's Brendaon, and my son-in-law is Joe. And I'm sitting next to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at here? Explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, here at the top there is the city Igarka, the North Pole. And to the side is the Ural Mountains, which is a, a big city built there in the Ural Mountains, they call Magnitogorsk. Magnitogorsk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And beside this is Siberia. The city is below Novosibirsk, Omsk, Tomsk, and Krasnoyarsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went all the way there south to Samarkand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From Igarka, I was free to go in the Polish army, and we went to Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was south?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From the North Pole to south.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Black Sea, almost? Almost to the Black Sea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Almost to the Black Sea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I, I didn't see the Black Sea. But I see the dark there. Nine months. The Eskimos live there, it's the North Pole. You can't go further. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] My-- my name is Sol Glick. My first name in Europe was Sol Galek. I got married here in 1953, and I have two wonderful daughters. One is with me here now. Her name is Lois. And I have three grandchildren, Howie Ptak, Nicole Ptak, and a little boy, 5 and 1/2 years, Eric Ptak. And I have a daughter in California. Her name is Brenda, and her husband is Joe, and their-- and their little boy, six years old, is Brendon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Howard, could you share something special you do with your grandfather? And what does-- what does your grandfather mean to you? Lean forward towards the mic.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my grandfather very much. And every time that I'm with him, I enjoy it as much I can. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he tell you a lot of stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he's told me stories. Good, bad, and funny. And they're very interesting and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nicole, what about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I also love my grandpa very much. And when he comes over, he tells me stories. And sometimes, we go to the movies and we do a lot of things together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a particular story that he tells you a lot that has made a difference in your life, or has influenced you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How he escaped from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lois, do you have something to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I love my father very much. I can't believe all the things that he's gone through. It's really amazing for me to believe that this really happened, living in America. And it's incredible that he survived this ordeal. And I wish more of my family survived. But I'm thankful for my cousins that I have. And I'm thankful that my dad's still here and can tell all these incredible stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eric, do you have something to tell us? No?

speaker: interviewer
text: Say your name, Eric.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you.
